walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Time Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Patchwork Express set up. First, place the time board in the center of the table where both players can reach it. This is the long track that measures how much time you have spent sewing your quilt. Both players will move along this track during the game.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Patches
walkthrough.text: Now take all twenty patch tiles and lay them out in a circle around the time board. Spread them out so there is a nice ring of patches surrounding the board. These are the fabric pieces you will be buying and adding to your quilt throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Neutral Token
walkthrough.text: Find the neutral token. Look through the ring of patches and find the smallest one, the patch with the lowest button cost. Place the neutral token directly after that patch going clockwise. This token marks where the available patches start each turn.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player Their Materials
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one quilt board, which is the seven-by-seven grid you will be filling with patches. Each player also takes one time token and places it on the starting space of the time board. Finally, give each player five buttons from the supply. Buttons are your currency for buying patches.

walkthrough.title: Determine the First Player
walkthrough.text: The player whose time token is further behind on the time track always goes next. Since both players start on the same space, the younger player goes first. Once you start playing, turns will alternate based on who is behind. You are all set to start sewing!

summary.title: Lay Out the Time Board
bullets: Place time board in center of table
Both players share this track

summary.title: Arrange the Patches
bullets: Lay all 20 patch tiles in a circle around the time board
These are the fabric pieces you'll buy during the game

summary.title: Place the Neutral Token
bullets: Find the smallest patch (lowest button cost)
Place neutral token just after it clockwise
Marks the start of available patches

summary.title: Give Each Player Their Materials
bullets: Each player: 1 quilt board (7x7 grid)
Each player: 1 time token on start of time track
Each player: 5 buttons as starting currency

summary.title: Determine the First Player
bullets: Player further behind on time track goes next
Both start equal — younger player goes first
Turns alternate based on who is behind

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Patchwork Express is a cozy two-player game where you compete to build the best quilt. On each turn, you either buy a patch to sew onto your quilt board or advance on the time track to earn buttons. The player with the most buttons at the end, minus penalties for empty spaces, wins.

walkthrough.title: Who Goes Next
walkthrough.text: This is important and a little different from most games. The player whose time token is further behind on the time track always takes the next turn. This means you might get several turns in a row if your opponent jumped far ahead. If both tokens are on the same space, the token on top goes first.

walkthrough.title: Option One: Buy a Patch
walkthrough.text: Look at the three patches directly ahead of the neutral token going clockwise. These are the only three you can buy. Pick one, pay its button cost from your supply, then move the neutral token to the spot where that patch was. Advance your time token on the time board by the number of spaces shown on the patch's time cost. Finally, place the patch on your quilt board. You can rotate or flip the patch, but it cannot overlap other patches or hang off the edge.

walkthrough.title: Option Two: Advance and Collect Buttons
walkthrough.text: If you do not want to buy a patch, or you cannot afford any of the three available patches, you can advance instead. Move your time token to the space directly in front of your opponent's token. Collect one button from the supply for each space you moved. This is a great way to earn money when the available patches are too expensive.

walkthrough.title: Button Income
walkthrough.text: Some patches have button icons printed on them. Whenever your time token passes over or lands on a button icon on the time track, you earn button income. Count up all the button icons on patches already placed on your quilt board and collect that many buttons from the supply. The more button-generating patches you have, the more income you earn each time you pass a button icon.

walkthrough.title: Special Single-Square Patches
walkthrough.text: Whenever your time token passes one of these spaces first, before your opponent, you take that single-square patch and immediately place it on your quilt board. These are free and help you fill in tricky gaps.

walkthrough.title: Placing Patches on Your Quilt
walkthrough.text: When you add a patch to your quilt board, you can rotate it and flip it however you like. The only rules are that it must fit entirely within the seven-by-seven grid and it cannot overlap any patch already on your board. Once a patch is placed, it cannot be moved. Think carefully about where each piece goes, because the shapes get harder to fit as your board fills up.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when both time tokens reach the final space of the time track. Once you reach the end, you stop and wait for your opponent to finish. Then both players count their remaining buttons and subtract two points for every empty space on their quilt board. The player with the higher score wins. If it is a tie, the player who reached the end of the time track first wins.

bullets: 2-player quilt-building game
Buy patches or advance for buttons
Highest score wins: buttons minus empty-space penalties

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Quilt
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening turns of a game between two players. We will call them Player A and Player B. Follow along at your own table to see how the game flows.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Both players start with five buttons and empty quilt boards. The neutral token is sitting in the patch circle, marking which three patches are available. Let's take a look at the first three patches clockwise from the neutral token. These are the only ones either player can buy right now.

walkthrough.title: Player A's First Turn: Buying a Patch
walkthrough.text: Player A looks at the three available patches and spots one that costs two buttons and has a button icon printed on it. That button icon means it will generate income later. Player A pays two buttons, takes the patch, moves the neutral token, advances on the time track, and places the patch on the quilt board. A great opening move!

walkthrough.title: Why Income Patches Matter Early
walkthrough.text: That patch with a button icon was an excellent first pick. Every time Player A crosses a button icon on the time track, that patch will earn one extra button. Buying income patches early means they trigger more times throughout the game. A patch bought on turn one might earn you four or five extra buttons over the whole game, while the same patch bought late might only earn one.

walkthrough.title: Player B's First Turn: The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player B sees a big patch among the three available options. It covers lots of squares, which seems great for filling the quilt. But it costs four buttons, and Player B only has five. Player B buys it anyway, leaving just one button. The patch has no button icon on it, so it will never generate income.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Risky
walkthrough.text: Player B spent almost all their buttons on a patch with no income. Now Player B has only one button left and might not be able to afford any patches next turn. When you spend all your buttons early on a patch that does not generate income, you end up forced to advance just to collect buttons. Meanwhile, your opponent is buying patches and building their quilt.

walkthrough.title: Player A's Second Turn: Smart Spending
walkthrough.text: Player A still has three buttons left and finds a cheap patch that costs just one button. It is a small piece, but it has a button icon on it. Player A buys it and places it on the quilt board. Now Player A has two patches with button icons, so the next time income triggers, Player A will earn two buttons at once.

walkthrough.title: Player B's Forced Advance
walkthrough.text: Player B checks the three available patches but cannot afford any of them with just one button. Player B is forced to advance on the time track, moving to the space just ahead of Player A's token. Player B collects one button per space moved. Advancing is not bad, but Player B lost a turn of quilt building because they overspent earlier.

walkthrough.title: The Placement Mistake
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player B has enough buttons to buy a new patch. But when trying to place it on the quilt board, Player B realizes they put the first big patch right in the center of the board. Now there are awkward corners and edges that this new patch shape cannot fill. Player B has to squeeze it into a less ideal spot, leaving gaps that will be hard to fill later.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Placement
walkthrough.text: When placing patches, think about the edges and corners of your board first. These are the hardest areas to fill later because patches can only fit shapes that stay inside the grid. Try to build outward from one corner rather than starting in the middle. Leaving a clean open area gives you more options for future patches.

walkthrough.title: Button Income Triggers
walkthrough.text: Player A's time token crosses a button icon on the time track. Income time! Player A counts the button icons on all patches placed on the quilt board. There are two, so Player A collects two buttons from the supply. Player B also crosses the same button icon later, but only has one button icon on the quilt, so Player B collects just one. This gap grows every time income triggers.

walkthrough.title: Grabbing a Special Patch
walkthrough.text: Player A's time token passes a space with a special single-square patch. Since Player A is the first to reach it, Player A takes the patch and immediately places it on the quilt board. These little one-by-one patches are free and perfect for plugging gaps. Always remember to claim them when you pass the space first.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Nice work! You have seen the core flow of Patchwork Express. You learned to buy income patches early, avoid overspending your buttons, plan your patch placement carefully, and claim special patches when you pass them. Remember, every empty space costs you two points at the end, so fill that quilt! You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Start Sewing
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to game terms and patch types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, spending all your buttons on one big patch early and having nothing left to buy with. Second, ignoring patches with button icons and missing out on recurring income. Third, placing patches in the center of the board and creating impossible-to-fill edges. Fourth, forgetting about the two-point penalty for every empty space. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Income Patches
walkthrough.text: Patches with button icons printed on them are your best friends early in the game. Every time your time token crosses a button icon on the time track, you earn income equal to the number of button icons on your quilt. A patch bought early with two button icons might earn you six to eight extra buttons over the course of the game. That is money that lets you buy more and better patches later. Always consider an income patch before a non-income patch of similar size.

walkthrough.title: Balance Time and Buttons
walkthrough.text: Every patch has two costs: buttons and time. A patch that costs one button but advances your time by six spaces might seem cheap, but you are burning a huge amount of game time. You want patches that give you good coverage without pushing your time token too far ahead. Small time costs let you take more turns overall, since the player further behind always goes next.

walkthrough.title: Fill Your Board
walkthrough.text: Every empty space on your quilt board costs you two points at the end of the game. On a seven-by-seven grid with forty-nine spaces, leaving even five spaces empty costs you ten points. That is a massive penalty. Always be thinking about coverage. Sometimes a patch that does not generate income but fills a tricky gap is worth more than an income patch that leaves you with holes.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Patch Placement
walkthrough.text: Before you place a patch, take a moment to think about what shapes you might need later. Try to build from corners and edges inward, since these areas are hardest to fill with oddly shaped pieces. Avoid leaving single-space holes surrounded on all sides unless you have a special single-square patch coming. A clean, open area on your board gives you the most flexibility for future patches.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Advance
walkthrough.text: Advancing instead of buying a patch is not a failure. Sometimes the three available patches are overpriced, poorly shaped, or just not what you need. Advancing earns you buttons and lets you pass over button income icons and special patch spaces on the time track. If advancing would trigger an income event or let you grab a special single-square patch, it might be the best play even if you could afford to buy something.

walkthrough.title: Watch Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Because the neutral token determines which three patches are available, buying one patch changes what your opponent can see. If you notice your opponent needs a specific shape or a valuable income patch, consider buying it first to deny them. Patchwork Express is a short game and every patch denied to your opponent is a small advantage. Also pay attention to their button supply. If they are low on buttons, the expensive patch in front of them might rotate to you for free.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Mindset
walkthrough.text: As both time tokens approach the end of the track, shift your thinking from income to coverage. Income patches that you buy near the end of the game will never trigger because there are no more button icons ahead of you on the track. In the final few turns, prioritize patches that fill the most empty spaces on your board, regardless of whether they have button icons. Every space you fill saves you two points of penalty.

bullets: Don't blow all buttons on one big patch early
Don't ignore income patches (button icons)
Don't place patches in the center first — build from edges
Remember: -2 points per empty space at game end

walkthrough.title: The Income Engine Opening
walkthrough.text: The strongest opening in Patchwork Express is to buy two or three cheap patches with button icons in your first few turns, even if they are small. This creates a compounding income engine. Each time you cross a button icon on the time track, you earn two or three buttons instead of zero. This surplus funds bigger, more coverage-heavy patches later in the game when you need to fill your board.

walkthrough.title: Time Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Because the player further behind on the time track always goes next, you can take multiple turns in a row by buying patches with low time costs. This is incredibly powerful. If you buy a patch that only advances your time by one space, you stay behind your opponent and get to go again immediately. Chaining several small-time patches together lets you grab three or four patches before your opponent gets a single turn.

walkthrough.title: Denying Key Patches
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to what your opponent needs. If they have a large gap on their board and one of the three available patches would fill it perfectly, consider buying that patch yourself, even if it is not ideal for your quilt. In a game where empty spaces cost two points each, denying your opponent a four-space patch effectively swings the score by eight points. This is especially powerful in the late game when options are running out.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Advancing
walkthrough.text: Advancing past your opponent is not just about earning buttons. Use it strategically to claim special single-square patches on the time track and to trigger button income events. Sometimes the best play is to advance past a button icon on the time track, collect your income, grab a special patch, and end up with more resources than any purchasable patch would have given you.

walkthrough.title: Corner and Edge Strategy
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about their quilt board as a puzzle from turn one. The corners of the seven-by-seven grid are the hardest to fill because patches can only approach from two directions. Start placing patches in one corner and build outward. Keep your open area as rectangular as possible so that any shape can still fit. The moment you create an isolated gap or a narrow corridor, your options shrink dramatically.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Patch Circle
walkthrough.text: Look beyond the three currently available patches. The neutral token moves to the spot where the purchased patch was, so you can predict which patches will become available after each purchase. If buying patch A would expose an amazing patch for your next turn, that changes the value of patch A dramatically. Think one or two moves ahead in the circle to maximize the value of every purchase.

walkthrough.title: When to Stop Buying Income
walkthrough.text: Income patches lose value as the game progresses because there are fewer button icons remaining on the time track ahead of you. Once you have passed the last button icon on the track, income patches are worth zero extra buttons. A good rule of thumb is to stop prioritizing income after you have passed the halfway point of the time track. From that point forward, focus entirely on coverage and filling empty spaces.

walkthrough.title: Evaluating Patch Value
walkthrough.text: To compare patches quickly, think about what you get versus what you pay. The best patches have a low button cost, a low time cost, good coverage in useful shapes, and ideally a button icon for income. The worst patches cost a lot of buttons and time but only cover two or three squares with no income. When in doubt, calculate the coverage per time cost. A patch covering four squares for two time is better than a patch covering three squares for four time.

summary.title: The Income Engine Opening
bullets: Buy 2-3 cheap income patches in first few turns
Creates compounding income at each button icon on track
Surplus buttons fund bigger coverage patches later
Small early income patches outperform large non-income patches

summary.title: Time Manipulation
bullets: Player behind always goes next — use this
Low-time patches let you chain multiple turns in a row
3-4 patches in a row before opponent moves = huge advantage
Time cost matters as much as button cost

summary.title: Denying Key Patches
bullets: Buy patches your opponent needs, even if imperfect for you
Denying a 4-space patch = 8-point score swing
Most powerful in late game when options are limited
Watch opponent's board for gaps they need to fill

summary.title: Strategic Advancing

Sometimes nets more resources than any purchasable patch
Use to jump past multiple button icons and special patches
Not just a fallback — can be the optimal play

entries.definition: The currency in Patchwork Express. Used to buy patches. Remaining buttons at game end count as victory points, one point per button.
The number of buttons you must pay from your supply to purchase a patch tile. Shown on the patch with a button icon and a number.

When your time token passes a button icon on the time track, you earn buttons equal to the number of button icons printed on all patches currently on your quilt board. More button patches means more income each trigger.
The central track shared by both players. Time tokens move along it when patches are purchased or when a player advances. The game ends when both tokens reach the end.
Each player's marker on the time board. The player whose token is further behind always takes the next turn. If tokens share a space, the one on top goes first.
The number of spaces your time token advances on the time board when you buy a patch. Shown on the patch with an hourglass icon and a number. Higher time cost means fewer future turns.
Each player's personal seven-by-seven grid where patches are placed. Empty spaces at game end cost two points each. Patches can be rotated and flipped but cannot overlap.

The marker in the patch circle that determines which three patches are available for purchase. Only the three patches immediately clockwise of this token can be bought. It moves to the spot of the purchased patch after each buy.
The ring of patch tiles arranged around the time board. Players buy patches from this circle. As patches are purchased, the circle shrinks and the neutral token shifts.
Free one-by-one patches placed on specific spaces of the time board. The first player to pass the space claims the patch and immediately places it on their quilt board. Great for filling small gaps.
One of two turn options. Instead of buying a patch, move your time token to the space directly ahead of your opponent's token. Collect one button per space moved from the supply.
One of two turn options. Choose one of the three patches ahead of the neutral token, pay its button cost, advance your time token by its time cost, and place the patch on your quilt board.
At game end, each uncovered space on your quilt board costs two victory points. On a seven-by-seven grid with forty-nine spaces, this penalty can be devastating if large areas remain unfilled.
Score equals remaining buttons minus two points per empty quilt space. Tiebreaker goes to the player who reached the end of the time track first.
